返回本批次

富有成效的拖延

Productive Procrastination

www.maxvanijsselmuiden.nl 作者 maxvij 分数 134 评论 45 2026/04/11 13:21
打开 HN 原帖 打开原始文章

中文摘要

  1. 过去几周，感觉拖延症无处不在。这可能只是频率错觉——但它在我的脑海里，所以我需要写下来。不久前，我在拍摄几天后在我的频道上完成了一段视频。尽管我的常规日程要求我上传以前旅行中的视频，但我将该视频伪装成一个特别的视频，以便在旅行附近进行处理，然后上传它。我喜欢它。这是富有成效的。发布了一个新视频。感觉很好。这并不是我绝对必须做的一件事：完成我的旧视频。如果这些视频能完成就太好了。我喜欢为他们工作。这是我喜欢的事情。但我还是回避它。如果我能理解这些问题的答案：
  2. 为什么从事新项目比现有项目更有趣？为什么我们要逃避我们实际上必须做的工作？
  3. 我也许能找到解决拖延问题的方法。生产力矩阵 Casey Neistat，我最喜欢的 YouTube 用户之一，最近制作了一个关于此的视频。我们都知道我们应该做 X，但我们做了 Y，才能感到富有成效。他提到了代表四个不同类别的生产力矩阵。将鼠标悬停或点击类别即可查看与该类别匹配的他的活动。我想做但绝对不应该做的事情 视频游戏 和 Sam Twitter 一起看 YouTube 我想做、绝对应该做的事情，但出于某种原因我避免做 制作 YouTube 视频 编辑 YouTube 视频 我不想做、不需要做但会做的事情，以避免我应该做的事情 检查邮箱重新布线电子设备 整理设备 清洁工作室 我不想做但绝对需要做的事情 工作电话 回复电子邮件 想做的事 不想做低效的高效工作 凯西的四象限生产力矩阵。将鼠标悬停或点击象限即可查看示例。这些类别确实是凯西风格的、描述性的和随意的，但他在这里提出了一个有效的观点。我们的物品都应该属于这些类别。这也发生在我身上——电子游戏、电视节目或行政管理，以避免我应该做的事情。但这种情况并不经常发生，而且不一定是我的问题。我仍然保持高效，并且我想在高效的同时做我所做的事情。所以根据这个矩阵，它属于右上方的类别。这很好。问题是我很有成效并且很有趣，但我仍然知道这不是我应该关注的事情。该矩阵中没有相应的类别。我们称之为富有成效的拖延。你的大脑正在保护你，让我们弄清楚这里发生了什么。在进行研究时，普遍的共识是拖延来自于我们大脑中两个系统之间的碰撞。第一个系统是边缘系统，或更具体地说是杏仁核，它是我们大脑的一个区域，在情绪的处理和记忆中起着核心作用。它处理威胁、奖励和情绪。第二个系统是前额皮质（PFC），它更加深思熟虑：计划、冲动控制和长期推理。因此，当我们不拖延时，前额叶皮层正在正常执行其任务：我们正在控制自己的冲动，按计划行事并进行长期思考。问题是，当一项任务引发显着的负面情绪（焦虑、无聊、害怕失败）时，杏仁核就会接管，我们会尽力避免这种负面情绪。所以，总的来说，这就是正在发生的事情。我们的大脑正在保护我们免受与该任务相关的负面情绪的影响。一个合乎逻辑的结论是解决与主要任务相关的负面情绪。奖励新奇但这还不是全部。大脑很复杂，有不同的系统在起作用。就我而言，我仍然富有成效，只是没有专注于我应该关注的任务。我相信这与任务的新颖性有关。这件事已经在我脑海里萦绕了一段时间了。为了满足我的视频编辑爱好，我制作摩托车旅行视频。基本上，视频博客。越接近实际日期，我就越有动力完成视频。第一次录制视频和我花在处理视频上的时间之间的时间越长，我的工作效率就越低。简而言之，我更喜欢处理新录制的视频。这就是我将最新视频伪装成特殊视频的部分原因。我还有很多视频要制作，按时间顺序排列，是关于我去南岛旅行的，距今已有 90 多天了。有趣的是我有数据来测试这个理论。这在科学上并不完全准确，但我们可能会看到一种趋势。而且，这样做很有趣，我在逃避我应该做的事情，所以让我们尝试一下吧。我收集了 20 个已发布视频的数据。在 20 个已发布的视频中，我将其中两个标记为特别视频 - 因为主题的不同足以打破时间顺序的模式。视频编辑每分钟的最终镜头大约需要一个小时，所以较长的视频自然需要更长的时间。这就是为什么我按持续时间进行标准化的原因。输出是持续时间（以分钟为单位）除以第一个记录日期和导出日期之间的增量。记录和导出之间的天数越多 = 每天的输出越低 图 1 显示，随着初始记录天数的增加，我的输出会下降。另请注意，特价商品的产量略高。在我的旅行期间，我有足够的时间来制作视频。旅行结束后，我于 1 月 19 日在 Sandfield 开始了一份全职工作。所以让我们将其添加到等式中。每个视频随时间的输出。虚线标志着全职工作的开始。一个简单的时间顺序图表显示，随着时间的推移，我制作视频的效率总体上下降了。这是有道理的，自从我再次开始每周工作 40 小时后，我专注于它的时间就更少了。但这两个特色菜绝对在这里脱颖而出，输出要高得多。从首次录制到导出的天数（按时间顺序）。黄色条表示特价，虚线表示全职工作的开始。因此，我对旧项目的兴趣不如对新项目的兴​​趣。我确信我不是唯一一个。采取了这个非科学的回避措施后，让我们看看实际的科学是怎么说的。2 科学怎么说？奖励系统 我们大脑的奖励系统主要集中在多巴胺上。正如舒尔茨在 1998 年发现的那样，多巴胺是根据奖励预测线索而释放的。立即可用的刺激比抽象的未来刺激产生更强的信号。更具体地说，邦泽克和杜泽尔证明，我们的大脑会对新奇刺激做出特定反应。这个想法是，新的刺激会触发海马到腹侧被盖区的循环，从而增强学习和记忆。魏特曼等人。 （2007）发现，即使是预测新刺激的线索也会激活我们的奖励系统。相反，熟悉的刺激会触发重复抑制，从而抑制我们的大脑对已经遇到的刺激的反应。我们的大脑经过训练可以对新刺激做出积极反应。因此，开始一个新项目会让人感到兴奋和激动。这一定就是为什么一个习惯很难长期坚持的原因，或者说为什么我个人总是喜欢穿对我来说新的衣服的原因。人类一直对新鲜事物感兴趣。它为我们指明了探索和创新的方向。只是现在对我没有帮助。避免内疚所以，我们已经找到了项目新颖性的答案。现在，我们该如何解释我因没有完成主要任务而感到的内疚呢？没有努力去做的内疚应该让我克服它，并真正开始，但也许真正开始会加剧内疚。这意味着我们正在面对一个无限循环，在没有取得进展的同时不断增加内疚感。也许我感到的内疚是与主要任务相关的负面情绪。道德许可过去的“好”行为会产生开始“坏”行为的心理许可（Monin 和 Miller，2001）。完成几项富有成效的任务（但不是主要任务）可以让我们相信我们已经完成了很多工作。过去几周在视频编辑之外确实富有成效：完整的产品组合重新设计和重组、振动编码的健康食谱网站以及本地人工智能实验的建立。在处理这些实际上富有成效的其他任务时，我的大脑会被欺骗，认为我一直很有成效——所以没有必要处理主要任务。蔡加尼克效应蔡加尼克效应指出，未完成的任务会持续存在于工作记忆中，从而产生认知紧张。我们的大脑对未完成的任务的记忆比对已完成的任务的记忆更好。它困扰着我们，未完成的任务。我正在逃避的任务正在消耗精神带宽，即使我在逃避它。马西坎波和鲍迈斯特的一项研究表明，为这些未完成的任务制定具体计划可以消除这种认知负担——当然，更好的解决办法是实际完成任务。反之亦然：完成偏差表明我们的大脑更喜欢完成小而简单的任务，而不是解决重要的困难任务。完成小任务时有积极的感觉，未完成较大任务时有消极的感觉。这就是愧疚感产生的原因。 解决方案 因此，现在我们已经找到了问题的两个主要答案，让我们看看如何解决它们。数据和科学指出了两个答案：我们的大脑更喜欢新奇的东西，它们保护我们免受与我们认为必须做的任务相关的负面情绪的影响。为了让旧项目的工作变得更有趣，我们必须引入刺激，让项目感觉像是新的。欺骗我们自己的大脑并不容易，所以我们必须真正找到创造性的方法，让旧的任务再次变得有趣。对我来说，它可以专注于我想尝试的新视频编辑技术。很难弄清楚每个新视频，所以我必须在解决方案上更有创意。但是，这是一个开始。情感标签 利伯曼及其同事的一项研究表明，标记情绪刺激会激活前额叶制动。拖延是由负面情绪驱动的，而明确的情绪会打破自动回避。原谅自己 Wohl、Pychyl 和 Bennett (2010) 进行的一项有趣的研究表明，原谅自己在期中考试中拖延的学生在下一次期中考试中的拖延现象显着减少。自我宽恕可以减少愧疚感。另一方面，自我批评会扩大我们大脑试图避免的负面情绪。养成习惯然后还有实用的技巧，将提示和任务结合起来（如果是上午 9 点，我坐在办公桌前，那么我会花 30 分钟完成主要任务）可以帮助您开始。无论如何，开始一项任务通常是最困难的部分。对我来说，了解心理学原理拖延背后有帮助。现在我明白了我的大脑就是让我有这种感觉，我不再感到内疚了。最后，你需要找到解决拖延症的方法。我希望阅读这些心理学原理能让您有更好的理解。我写了这篇文章，所以对我来说，这已经是富有成效的一天。或者是吗？
  4. 使用 Chart.js 构建的图表。 ↩
  5. 虽然本文中的每个单词都是由我（一个人）写的，但我使用 Claude 作为语法、拼写错误和研究助理。 ↩

Original Summary

  1. The past few weeks, it feels like procrastination is everywhere. It might just be the frequency illusion — but it’s on my mind, so I need to write about it. Not too long ago, I finished a video on my channel, a couple of days after filming. Even though my regular schedule would require me to upload videos from an older trip, I disguised the video as a special to work on it close to the trip and just upload it. And I loved it. It was productive. A new video got published. It felt good. It just wasn’t the one thing I absolutely have to do: finish my older videos. It would be great to have those videos finished. I enjoy working on them. It’s a thing I love. Yet I avoid it. If I can understand the answer to these questions:
  2. Why is it more fun to work on a new project than one that’s been around? Why do we avoid the work we actually have to do?
  3. I might be able to figure out a way to solve my procrastination issue. The productivity matrix Casey Neistat, one of my favourite YouTubers, recently made a video about this. We all know we should be doing X but we do Y, to feel productive. He mentions a productivity matrix representing four different categories. Hover or tap the categories to see his activities that match the category. things I want to do but should absolutely not do Video gamesHang w/ SamTwitterWatch YouTube things I want to do and absolutely should do but for some reason I avoid doing Make YouTube videosEdit YouTube videos things I don't want to do and don't need to but will do to avoid the thing I should do Check mailboxRewire electronicsOrganize gearClean studio things I don't want to do but absolutely need to do Work callsRespond to emails want to do don't want to do unproductive productive Casey's four-quadrant productivity matrix. Hover or tap a quadrant to see examples. The categories are really Casey-style, descriptive and casual, but he makes a valid point here. We should all have our items falling into each of these categories. It happens to me too — video games, TV shows or administration, to avoid the thing I should be doing. But that doesn’t really happen a lot, and isn’t necessarily my problem here. I’m still being productive, and I want to do the things I do while being productive. So according to this matrix, that would fall in the top right category. Which is good. The issue is that I’m productive and having fun, but still, I’m aware it’s not the thing I should be focusing on. There’s no category for that in this matrix. Let’s call it productive procrastination. Your brain is protecting you Let’s figure out what’s happening here. When doing research, the general consensus is that procrastination comes from a collision between two systems in our brains. The first system being the limbic system, or more specifically the amygdala, an area of our brains which plays a central role in the processing and memory of emotions. It processes threats, rewards and emotions. The second system is the prefrontal cortex (PFC) which is more deliberate: planning, impulse control and long-term reasoning. So, when we’re not procrastinating, the prefrontal cortex is doing its task properly: we’re controlling our impulses, acting as planned and thinking long-term. The problem is when a task triggers a significant negative emotion — anxiety, boredom, fear of failure — the amygdala takes over and we try to avoid this negative emotion. So, in general, that’s what’s happening. Our brains are protecting us from the negative emotions related to that task. A logical conclusion would be to address the negative emotions associated with the main task. Rewarding novelty But that can’t be all. Brains are complicated, there’s different systems in play here. In my case, I am still being productive, just not on the tasks that I should be focusing on. I believe this has to do with the novelty of the tasks. It’s something that’s been on the back of my mind for a while. For my video editing hobby I make motorcycle travel videos. Vlogs, basically. The closer to the actual date, the more motivated I am to finish a video. The more time there is between the first recording of a video and the time I spend working on the video, the less efficient I work. Simply put, I prefer working on newly recorded videos. Which is partially why I disguised my latest video as a special video. I’ve still got many videos to make, chronologically, about a trip I made to the South Island, which is over 90 days ago by now. The fun part is that I’ve got the data to test this theory. It’s not exactly scientifically accurate, but we might see a trend. Also, it’s fun to do, I’m avoiding what I should be doing, so let’s give it a go. I’ve gathered the data for my 20 published videos. Out of the 20 published videos, two I’ve marked as specials — because the topic was different enough to break the chronological pattern. Video editing takes me about an hour per minute of final footage, so longer videos should naturally take longer. Which is why I normalise by duration. The output is the duration in minutes divided by the delta between first recorded date and the export date. More days between recording and export = lower output per day The chart1 shows that my output drops as the amount of days from the initial recording increases. Also note that the specials have a slightly higher output. During my trip, I had all the time in the world to work on the videos. After my trip I started a full time job at Sandfield on January 19. So let’s add that to the equation. Output per video over time. The dashed line marks the start of full-time work. A simple chronological chart shows that over time I have become less productive in making videos in general. It makes sense, since I started working 40 hours per week again, there’s less time for me to focus on it. But the two specials definitely stand out here, the output is much higher. Days from first recording to export, in chronological order. Yellow bars are specials, the dashed line marks the start of full-time work. So, I’ve been less interested in working on the older projects than working on the newer projects. I’m sure I’m not the only one. Having taken this non-scientific side-step, let’s take a look at what the actual science has to say.2 What does science say? Reward system Our brain’s reward system is mainly focused around dopamine. Dopamine is released in response to reward-predictive cues, as found by Schultz in 1998. Immediately available stimuli generate stronger signals than abstract future stimuli. More specifically, Bunzeck and Düzel demonstrated that our brain responds specifically to stimulus novelty. The idea is that novel stimuli trigger a hippocampal to ventral tegmental area loop enhancing learning and memory. Wittmann et al. (2007) found that even cues predicting novel stimuli activate our reward system. In contrast, familiar stimuli trigger repetition suppression, dampening our brain’s response to stimuli we’ve already encountered. Our brains are trained to respond positively to new stimuli. Starting a new project feels exciting and thrilling because of this. This must be the reason why it’s hard to stick to a habit for a longer period, or why I personally always prefer wearing clothes that are new to me. Humans have always been interested in the new. It has pointed us towards exploration and innovation. It’s just not helping me right now. Avoiding guilt So, we’ve found an answer to the novelty of the projects. Now, how can we explain the guilt I feel for not working on the main task. The guilt of not working on it should make me overcome it, and actually start, but perhaps actually starting reinforces the guilt. This would mean we’re dealing with an infinite cycle, ever-increasing the feeling of guilt while not making progress. Perhaps the guilt I feel is the negative emotion associated with the main task. Moral licensing Past ‘good’ behaviour generates the psychological permission to start ‘bad’ behaviour (Monin and Miller, 2001). Completing several productive tasks (but not the main task), can trick us into believing we’ve already accomplished a lot. The past few weeks were genuinely productive outside of video editing: a full portfolio redesign and restructure, a vibe-coded healthy recipe website, and a local AI experiment set up. Working on these other tasks that are actually productive, my brain gets deceived into thinking that I’ve been productive — so there’s no need to work on the main task. Zeigarnik Effect The Zeigarnik Effect states that unfinished tasks persist in working memory, creating cognitive tension. Our brains are remembering the unfinished tasks better than the finished ones. It bothers us, unfinished tasks. The task I’m avoiding is consuming mental bandwidth even while I’m avoiding it. A study by Masicampo and Baumeister states that making a specific plan for these unfinished tasks eliminates this cognitive burden — of course, the better fix is to actually finish the task. The opposite is true as well: completion bias states that our brains have a preference for finishing small and easy tasks over tackling important difficult ones. A positive feeling for completing small tasks, a negative feeling for not completing bigger tasks. That’s why the guilt sets in. The solution So, now that we’ve found the two main answers to the questions, let’s look at what to do about them. The data and the science point to two answers: our brains prefer novelty, and they protect us from the negative emotions tied to tasks we feel we have to do. To make it more fun to work on an older project, we have to introduce stimuli to make the project feel as if it is new. Tricking our own brains is not easy, so we’ll genuinely have to find creative ways to make an older task feel interesting again. For me, it can be focusing on a new video editing technique that I want to try out. Hard to figure out for each new video, so I’ll have to be more creative with my solutions here. But, it’s a start. Affect labeling A study by Lieberman and colleagues shows that labeling emotional stimuli activates the prefrontal brake. Procrastination is driven by negative affect, and making the affect explicit breaks the automatic avoidance. Forgive yourself An interesting study by Wohl, Pychyl and Bennett (2010) shows that students who forgave themselves for procrastinating on a midterm significantly reduced procrastination on the following midterm. Self-forgiveness reduces the guilt. Self-criticism, on the other hand, expands the negative emotions that our brains are trying to avoid. Make it a habit Then there are the practical tips, combining a cue and a task (if it’s 9 AM and I sit at my desk, then I work on the main task for 30 min) can get you started. Starting a task often is the hardest part anyway. For me, understanding the psychological principles behind procrastination helps. I feel less guilt, now that I understand that my brain is wired to make me feel this way. In the end, it’s up to you to figure out a way to resolve procrastination. I hope reading about these psychological principles has given you a better understanding. I’ve written this article, so for me, it’s already a productive day. Or is it?
  4. Charts built with Chart.js. ↩
  5. While each word in this article was written by me, a human, I’ve used Claude as a grammar, typo and research assistant. ↩

评论（默认中文）

gobdovan 2026/04/11 19:26
我有一些处理拖延症的技巧：

1. 当我看到自己想要拖延时，我问自己“如果我遵循这种感觉，它会增加我的力量（即能力/能动性/效用）还是会减少？”。然后我与自己对话：不，让我们重新集中注意力，也许尝试大声朗读或画图表或其他一些视角改变，或者是的，我现在应该停下来，做点别的事情，只要这能增加我的力量。

2. 我观察到，通常拖延确实与新颖性有关，这与文章中的介绍非常相似，所以我做了这样的事情：我开始在线打字练习，而不是上 YouTube 或游戏。一段时间后，我意识到我可以通过打字现有的书来提高打字速度并获得一些额外的新奇！所以我有一个 Tampermonkey 脚本，每当我尝试进入随机打字网站时，它会将我重定向到一个可以打字的网站（如果有人感兴趣，我可以将其作为要点推送）。它在本地存储中存储我到达的页面以及我离开的页面。我以这种方式阅读了《物种起源》，现在我的打字速度从 80 WPM 提高到了 100 WPM 左右。

I have a few tricks for handling procrastination that are in this ballpark:

1. When I see myself wanting to procrastinate, I ask myself 'If I follow this feeling, will it increase my power (i.e. capacity/agency/utility) or decrease it?'. Then I have a dialogue with myself: Nope, let's refocus, maybe try reading things out loud or draw a diagram or some other perspective change OR Yeah, I should stop for now, do something else, as long as that increases my power.

2. I observed that usually procrastination really is tied to novelty, quite similar with how it's presented in the article so I did this thing: instead of going on YouTube or games I started typing exercises online. After some time, I realised that I could get better at typing and get some extra-novelty by typing an existing book! So I have a Tampermonkey script that, whenever I try to go on a random typing website, redirects me to a website where I can type books (I could push it as a gist if anyone's interested). It stores in Local Storage what page I reached and from where I left them of. I got to read On the Origin of Species this way and now I type around 100 WPM from 80 WPM.

在 HN 中查看
sebmellen 2026/04/12 02:01
您能分享 Tampermoneky 脚本的要点吗？会喜欢的。
Could you share the Tampermoneky script in the gist? Would love that.
在 HN 中查看
vscode-rest 2026/04/11 21:23
但是您对物种起源了解多少？
But how much have you learned about the Origin of Species?
在 HN 中查看
gobdovan 2026/04/11 22:07
相当多。我想说，我通过打字处理的信息比简单阅读的信息要多。我打出了第一版，之后也得到了印刷版。

我总是搜索他所举例的视频，并发现许多令人惊奇的材料（奴役蚂蚁、蚂蚁挠蚜虫、蜂窝结构）。 听到达尔文的同龄人每次都喊出名字，让我印象深刻，有人专门从事繁殖竞赛，判断物种的构成。

有点惊讶地发现人们不知道狗都是同一物种，即使是专业饲养员也很难识别他们的鸽子正在发生变化，因为他们并没有真正拍照。

达尔文如何出版了一本普通人可以理解的书，这本书是如何建立在大量手工收集的数据之上的。

我可以谈论的还有很多很多（生命之树、器官、同一年龄发生的后代相似性、胚胎学怪异），但最令人困惑的是他所持的反目的论立场。基本上，达尔文不知从何而来（虽然我看到他读过休谟[0]），发现事情的发生并不是“有原因的”。事物存在并不是因为它们“更好”。我们今天看到的所有生物都只是幸存下来的东西。世界上没有最终目标，也没有“应该”。我们只是幸存下来的模式，类似于碰巧幸存下来的模式。

[0] https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/hume/

Quite a lot. And I'd say that I process more info by typing than by simply reading. I typed the first edition and got the printed second edition too after.

I always searched videos of what he was exemplifying and found quite amazing material for many (enslaver ants, ants tickling aphids, honeycomb construction). Was super impressed to hear about Darwin's peers which he calls out by name every time, how there were people specialised in breading races, judging what constitutes species.

Was kind of stunned to find out that people didn't know dogs were all the same species, how hard it was even for specialised breeders to identify that their pigeons were changing, since they were not really taking pictures.

How Darwin published a book that was approachable to common folks, how the book was built on mountains of hand-collected data.

There's so, so, so much more I could talk about (tree of life, organs, descendant resemblance happening at the same age, embryology weirdness) but biggest mind-fuck would be the anti-teleological stance he holds. Basically, out of nowhere (although I saw that he read Hume [0]), Darwin figures out that things don't happen 'for a reason'. Things don't live because they're 'better'. All the creatures we see today are simply the things that survived. There's no final goal, no 'ought to be' in the world. We're simply patterns that survive that resemble patterns that happened to survive.

[0] https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/hume/

在 HN 中查看
pxc 2026/04/12 00:26
是的，请分享！
Yes, please share it!
在 HN 中查看
gobdovan 2026/04/12 02:56

https://gist.github.com/ouatu-ro/5ca4abca26bd65630de3d4768fe...
在 HN 中查看
pxc 2026/04/12 03:44
有时我也喜欢在开始项目之前使用打字测试页面来热身。我想做得非常好，与别人比赛，或者比平时打字更快，然后乘着胜利的高潮完成某件事或打破拖延。但这是一种更好的做事方式；这样我就可以让这项活动帮助推进我的其他目标。
I also sometimes like to use typing test pages to kinda warm myself up before I start a project. I want to do really well and race someone else or type faster than I usually can, then ride the high of victory to get something done or break procrastination. But this is a much better way to do things; this way I can make that activity help advance my other goals.
在 HN 中查看
laughing_abder 2026/04/12 01:16
这篇文章将新颖性和回避性视为独立的驱动因素，但从两个角度来看它们是一种机制。富有成效的副业任务不仅新颖。他们很安全。他们会生成能力证据，而不会冒着对真正重要的事情做出裁决的风险。

时间贴现数据是最有趣的部分，因为它揭示了文章中未提及的一些内容：任务越老，它就越容易从“我选择做的工作”转变为“我应该已经完成​​的工作”。这将其情感特征从机会转变为义务，而无论你是否喜欢潜在的工作，义务都会引发回避。

蔡加尼克的观点也违背了它自己的框架。多个富有成效的副项目会创建多个开环，所有这些都与主要任务争夺相同的工作内存。 “富有成效的”拖延不仅仅是逃避主要任务。它正在积极地分割主要任务所需的注意力预算。

The article identifies novelty and avoidance as separate drivers, but they're one mechanism viewed from two angles. The productive side tasks aren't just novel. They're safe. They generate evidence of competence without risking a verdict on the thing that actually matters.

The temporal discounting data is the most interesting part because it reveals something the article doesn't quite name: the older a task gets, the more it shifts from "work I'm choosing to do" to "work I should have already done." That transforms its emotional signature from opportunity to obligation, and obligations trigger avoidance regardless of whether you enjoy the underlying work.

The Zeigarnik point works against its own framing too. Multiple productive side projects create multiple open loops, all competing for the same working memory as the main task. The "productive" procrastination isn't just avoiding the main task. It's actively fragmenting the attention budget that the main task needs.

在 HN 中查看
maxvij 2026/04/12 06:12
你是对的，在我的初稿中，我也回避了义务方面的问题，但这最终导致文章过于分散。这绝对是它的相关部分，我喜欢的同样的任务一旦我有义务去做，就会变成一件苦差事。我喜欢你在评论中的措辞方式，改变了它的情感特征——说得很好。就是这样！
You're right, in my initial draft, I also took a side-step into figuring out the obligation aspect, but this ended up scattering the article too much. It's definitely a related part of it, the same tasks I love will become a chore as soon as I am obligated to do them. I love how you phrased it in your comment, transforming its emotional signature - very well put. That's what it is!
在 HN 中查看
softwaredoug 2026/04/12 03:03
我最近听到这样一句话：“如果你等到最后一分钟，那只需要你一分钟！”

对于我有的一些任务，我 需要 这样生活。专注于下周的任务会影响我现在正在做的事情。我必须相信自己有能力有效地将其整合在一起。当它到期时，我的脑海中常常需要对这项工作保持新鲜感——而不是几周前的东西。

有时这意味着意想不到的深夜。但大部分都已经解决了。

I recently heard the saying “if you wait to the last minute, it’ll only take you a minute!”

For some tasks I have, I need to live this way. Obsessing over a task next week takes away from what I’m doing now. I have to trust in my ability to pull it together efficiently. And when it’s due, the work often needs to be fresh in my mind - not something from weeks ago.

Sometimes that means unexpected late nights. But it’s mostly worked out.

在 HN 中查看
senko 2026/04/11 22:15
我相信每个人对此都有不同的策略，所以这是我的策略，以防它对某人有用：

我注意到，当我拖延时，它仍然给我带来压力，通常（随着时间的推移累积）比最终完成任务的实际不适感更大。现在只要有机会，精神力充足，我就会立即去完成任务，所以我不用担心。基本上我已经训练自己想要避免拖延引起的压力。只有当我有足够的精神能量来完成任务时它才有效。

我使用的第二个技巧，对于更大或更具（被认为）具有挑战性的事情，是对自己说，我今天就开始它并让它滚动，所以我对于完全避免它并不感到难过。有时，我会得意忘形并完成它，但即使没有，它仍然更容易拿起它（不久之后） - 无论如何都是胜利。

I believe everyone has a different strategy for this, so here’s mine in case it’s useful to someone:

I noticed that when I procrastinate, it still weighs on my mind, often more (cumulatively over time) than the actual discomfort of doing the task in the end. Now whenever I have the chance and enough mental energy, I attack the task immediately, so I don’t have to worry about it. Basically I've trained myself to want to avoid procrastination-induced stress. It only works if I have enough mental energy for the task tho.

The second trick I use, for bigger or more (perceived) challenging things, is saying to myself I’ll just start it and get it rolling today, so I don’t feel bad about completely avoiding it. Sometimes, I get carried away and just complete it, but even if not, it’s still easier to pick it up (a short while later) - a win in any case.

在 HN 中查看
codersfocus 2026/04/12 03:05
我认为寻求新奇是拖延的症状而不是原因。

如果你处于冲动状态，这就是导致拖延的原因。冲动显然会导致寻求新奇事物。

以下是我对动作循环进行建模的方法（顶部的图像）： https://wisedayplanner.com/blog/action-loop-impulsive-vs-eff...

I think novelty-seeking is a symptom rather than cause of procrastination.

If you're in an impulsive mode, that's what causes procrastination. Being impulsive will obviously lead to novelty seeking.

Here's how I model the action loop (image at the top): https://wisedayplanner.com/blog/action-loop-impulsive-vs-eff...

在 HN 中查看
miika 2026/04/12 01:35
有时拖延意味着我什至不应该这样做。也许我只是无意中听到了一个听起来“我应该做”的想法，但仅此而已。

我是如何解决这个问题的：我对自己说“我永远不会这样做”，无论这句话是真是假，我都会立刻感觉到。然后我要么立即开始，要么放手。

Sometimes procrastination means I’m not even suppose to do it. Maybe I just overheard an idea that sounded something “I should do” but there is nothing more to it.

How I hack this: I say to myself “I’m never going to do it” and there is an instant feeling if that statement is true or not. Then I either start it right away or let it go.

在 HN 中查看
andai 2026/04/11 17:48
很棒的文章，特别欣赏这些图表。 “不断增加新颖性”的想法可能是我成功的长期项目与不成功的项目的区别。

我之前将其归因于拥有大量的多样性和自由度，但这些因素的结果确实是新颖的。

我想提一下尼尔·菲奥雷（Neil Fiore）的优秀著作《当下的习惯》（The Now Habit），这是一本克服拖延症的实用手册。核心论文是用“我必须”这样的语言训练自己摆脱受害者心态，并用“我选择”这样的语言进入生产者心态。

对我来说有趣的是，这不是一个任意的选择。 “我必须”实际上是一种错觉。

想想最极端的情况。有人拿着枪“强迫”你炸毁学校。你“必须”这样做吗？或者说“不”会更好吗？

如果即使在最极端的情况下这种自由仍然存在……它不是总是存在吗？

有时你的选择确实很糟糕，但你总是有选择的。

这听起来可能太哲学化了，但我认为这是在日常生活中学习识别的一个重要区别。

因为未能认识到这一点是支持这种“我必须”的错觉的原因，这似乎是拖延的主要原因：对感知到的自主权丧失的不满和抵制。

所以我的意思是这不仅仅是更多 有用 以这种方式思考，作为某种心理技巧，但实际上它是 更真实 以及。

Great article, especially appreciated the graphs. The idea of "keep adding novelty" is probably what separated my successful long term projects from the unsuccessful ones.

I previously attributed that to having lots of variety and freedom, but the consequence of those factors was indeed novelty.

I want to mention Neil Fiore's excellent book The Now Habit, which is a practical manual on overcoming procrastination. The core thesis is training yourself out of the Victim Mindset, with language like "I have to", and into the Producer Mindset, with language like "I choose to."

What's interesting to me is that this isn't an arbitrary choice. "I have to" is actually a delusion.

Think of the most extreme scenario. Someone has a gun and is "forcing" you to blow up a school. Do you "have to" do it? Or would it be better to say no?

If that freedom holds even in the most extreme scenario... doesn't it always hold?

Sometimes your options are truly terrible, but you always have a choice.

That might sound too philosophical, but I think that's an important distinction to learn to recognize in everyday life.

Because the failure to recognize it is what supports this delusion of "I have to", which seems to be the main cause of procrastination: the resentment and pushing against perceived loss of autonomy.

So my meaning here is that it isn't just more useful to think this way, as some psychological trick, but that it is actually more true as well.

在 HN 中查看
andai 2026/04/11 17:52
另外，死机器人关于取消安排的评论是正确的！同一本书中的另一种技术:)

我使用它，发现它对心理健康有巨大的好处，至少对于我这种倾向于工作的性格类型来说是这样。

在“取消安排”（又名“无负罪感游戏时间”）中，您故意留出时间进行愉快的活动。您将它们放入日历中。 （然后你就真的去做了！）

这就消除了怨恨的一个主要原因，即“生活就是工作，没有娱乐”，这种怨恨会驱使人们对工作产生心理抵触。

当我说到这里时，我会再提一个:) 担心的工作......对于你正在避免的情况，有意地经历最坏的情况，然后意识到，实际上，我仍然会没事。即使那种可怕的情况发生……我会活下去，我会继续前进，我会没事的。

Also the dead bot comment is right about the Unschedule! Another technique from the same book :)

I use that one and have found it provides a massive benefit to mental health, at least for my personality type which tends to be consumed with work.

In the "Unschedule", a.k.a. Guilt-Free Play Time, you deliberately set away time for enjoyable activities. You put them in your calendar. (And then you actually do them!)

This removes a major cause of resentment, "life's all work and no play" which drives that psychological resistance to work.

While I'm at it, I'll mention one more :) The Work of Worrying... for a situation you're avoiding, intentionally go through the worst case scenario, and then realize, actually, I'll still be okay. Even if that terrible situation happens... I'll survive, I'll move on, I'll be okay.

在 HN 中查看
maxvij 2026/04/12 06:17
很高兴您喜欢这些图表。它们是我最喜欢的部分。

我研究了《当下的习惯》，并将其添加到我的阅读清单中。很好的补充，谢谢！尝试将“我必须”改写为“我选择”已经是我尝试过的事情，但最终，只有当你真正内化它时，这才会发生——如果你不真正相信你正在选择它，它就不会真正起作用（至少对我来说）。正如你所说，这是一种错觉。

正如文章中提到的，本质上它是变相的影响标签。

Glad you liked the graphs. They're my favourite part.

I looked into The Now Habit, and added it to my reading list. Great addition, thanks! Trying to rephrase 'I have to' to 'I choose to' is already something I try, but in the end, this only lands when you've genuinely internalised it - if you don't truly believe you're choosing it, it doesn't really work (at least for me). As you say, it's a delusion.

Essentially it's affect labelling in disguise, as mentioned in the article.

在 HN 中查看
nonameiguess 2026/04/11 20:37
很有趣的是看到让·保罗·萨特在这里长大。尽管他说得很啰嗦，但我确实同意这个框架。抛开基本的物理悖论和自由意志的不连贯性，在主观体验的层面上，所采取的每一个行动都是一种选择，可能是在极其糟糕的选择之间，但仍然是一种选择，而拥有它是我发现对生活保持理智的唯一方法。

也就是说，我不确定新奇的事情。我认为我一生中最伟大的长期计划是在年近 50 岁的时候保持健康、运动和健康，尽管遭受了一些可怕的伤害和挫折，并且迄今为止保持着相当幸福的婚姻。在这两种追求中，新颖性几乎是成功的前提。人们总是跳来跳去，永远不会进步，用一夜情和连续出轨来代替任何形式的终生关系。对我来说，这只是习惯的形成和基本的纪律，试图时刻提醒自己什么才是真正重要的。哎呀，这甚至可能是恐惧和其他东西一样多。我知道，如果我孤独且不健康，无论我在其他方面取得了什么成就，我都会在 60 多岁和 70 多岁的时候受到极大的伤害，如果我等到那时才尝试将一生的追求塞进一个十年，这将比简单地从 20 多岁开始，每周做一点，并坚持下去要困难得多，尽管有时可能会很辛苦，因为我知道这对我的未来意味着什么我必须做出选择，未来的我和现在的我一样重要。

相比之下，我不相信持续上传大量视频到 YouTube 是那么重要，但当然这个人可以自由地拥有自己的优先事项。

Interesting to effectively see Jean Paul Sartre being brought up here. As verbose as he was, though, I do agree with the framing. Putting aside the fundamental physical paradoxes and incoherence of free will, at the level of subjective experience, every action ever taken is a choice, possibly between extremely shitty options, but a choice nonetheless, and owning that is the only way I've ever found to stay sane about life.

That said, I'm not sure about the novelty thing. I'd rate the greatest long term project in my life as being staying fit, athletic, and healthy as I near 50, in spite of some horrible injuries and setbacks, and remaining thus far in a reasonably happy marriage. In both of those pursuits, novelty is almost the anthesis to success. People program hop and never improve, and substitute one-night stands and serial cheating for any form of lifelong relationship. To me, it is just habit-formation and basic discipline, trying to always remind myself what truly matters. Heck, it's probably even fear as much as anything else. I know I'm going to be hurting terribly in my 60s and 70s if I'm alone and unhealthy, regardless of what else I may have achieved, and if I wait until then to try and cram lifetime pursuits into a single decade, it'll be a lot harder than simply starting in my 20s, doing a little bit every week, and sticking with it in spite of how much of a grind it might be at times, because I know how much it will mean to future me and I have to make the choice that future me matters just as much as present me.

In contrast, I'm not convinced that consistently uploading a lot of videos to YouTube is all that important, but of course this guy is free to have his own priorities.

在 HN 中查看
maxvij 2026/04/12 06:29
> 也就是说，我不确定这是否新颖。我认为我一生中最伟大的长期计划是在年近 50 岁的时候保持健康、运动和健康，尽管遭受了一些可怕的伤害和挫折，并且迄今为止保持着相当幸福的婚姻。在这两种追求中，新颖性几乎是成功的前提。

你是对的，诸如此类的长期目标根本不会因新奇而蓬勃发展。我认为它们属于不同的类别，更多的是一种承诺，而不是一个需要拖延的一次性项目。

就我个人而言，我有类似的目标，而且我根本不为拖延与健身相关的任务而苦苦挣扎。现在我想起来，这是一个有趣的见解。那是因为我心中有一个长期目标吗？因为我知道这对我的健康很重要？

> That said, I'm not sure about the novelty thing. I'd rate the greatest long term project in my life as being staying fit, athletic, and healthy as I near 50, in spite of some horrible injuries and setbacks, and remaining thus far in a reasonably happy marriage. In both of those pursuits, novelty is almost the anthesis to success.

You're right that long-term goals such as these don't thrive on novelty at all. I suppose they fall in a different category, more of a commitment than a one-off project to be procrastinated about.

For me personally, I have similar goals, and I'm not struggling with procrastinating my fitness-related tasks at all. It's an interesting insight, now that I think about it. Would that be because I have a long-term goal in mind? Because I know it's important to my health?

在 HN 中查看
dbdr 2026/04/11 22:18
> 基本的物理悖论和自由意志的不连贯性

这些都是真的吗？它们确实存在于经典的牛顿物理学中，但它们是否存在于现代物理学中，例如物理学？量子物理学？并不是说一个人证明了自由意志，但真的存在一种绝对不可能的情况吗？

> the fundamental physical paradoxes and incoherence of free will

Are those even true? They sure are in classical, Newtonian physics, but are they in modern, e.g. quantum physics? Not saying that one proves free will, but is there an actual hard impossibility?

在 HN 中查看
rulesmen 2026/04/11 19:24
整个问题似乎更多的是一个容易分心的问题。一方面是因为穴居人的大脑将视线移开，不处理负面情绪，另一方面是因为它很容易从新的闪亮物体上转移。这是一个有效抛光焦点的问题。

佛教僧侣只是为了这个目的而做曼陀罗。然后他们随后销毁它，整个目的是为了获得一些代表训练的重点。

The whole of it seems more a question of an easily distracted mind. For one side because caveman brain looks away and does not adress the negative emotion , from the other because it's easily diverted from new shiny object. It's a question of effectively polish focus.

Budhist monks would simply do a mandala for the sake of it. Then they destroy it afterward, the whole purpose is to get some reps training focus.

在 HN 中查看
dmitrig01 2026/04/12 02:08
我讨厌在现代世界，我们沉迷于“高效”。为什么这很重要？为什么我们会因为“没有生产力”而惩罚自己？也许我们应该尝试将尽可能多的时间花在没有生产力或无法量化的事情上。在海滩度过一个轻松的下午是否“富有成效”？

我们已经失去了“存在”的能力。我们必须始终“做”。令人悲伤的状况。

I hate that in our modern world, we are obsessed with being "productive". Why is this important? Why do we punish ourselves for being "unproductive"? Maybe we should try and spend as much of our time in things that are unproductive or unquantifiable as possible. Is having a relaxing afternoon the beach "productive"?

We've lost our ability to just "be". We must always "do". A sad state of affairs.

在 HN 中查看
maxvij 2026/04/12 06:20
我明白为什么会出现这样的回应，但我只是想简单地说明一下，这根本不是我想要表达的观点。

我根本不沉迷于提高工作效率，这只是我一直在想的事情——而且我对它的心理学很感兴趣。我的很多日子都充满了我非常喜欢的非生产性活动。我只是不在我的职业相关博客上写这件事。所以我想这都是关于上下文的。

I see why this response came to be, but I just wanted to briefly annotate that this was not my intended point at all.

I'm not obsessed with being productive at all, it's just something that has been on my mind - and I'm interested in the psychology of it. Many of my days are filled with non-productive activities which I thoroughly enjoy. I just don't write about it on my profession-related blog. So I guess it's all about the context.

在 HN 中查看
oreoftw 2026/04/12 02:15
也许它感觉很重要/必要，因为它可以给人一种控制感。

事情是不确定的（它们确定过吗？），每天都有另一个世界末日的预言从社交媒体和报纸上向你尖叫。

因此，一个人可以尝试对冲，榨取更多的精力，以确保他做了他能做的一切。

Maybe it’s feels important/necessary as it can give a feeling of control.

Things are uncertain(were they ever certain?), every day there is another armageddon prophecy screaming at you from social media and newspapers.

So one can try to hedge, squeeze more out of oneself to make sure he did all he could.

在 HN 中查看
Chrisszz 2026/04/11 20:19
我明白了，但我仍然相信，提高生产力的最有效答案是纪律，所有欺骗的技巧（抱歉用了文字游戏）都只是从我们的工作中夺走了精力
I get it but I still believe that the most efficient answer to productivity is discipline, all the tricks to trick (sorry for the word play) are just effort taken away from our work
在 HN 中查看
embedding-shape 2026/04/11 20:20
我仍然相信，提高生产力的最有效答案是不工作和大量休息，我永远不会比闲置一段时间后达到更高的生产力。
I still believe the most efficient answer to productivity is not working and lots of resting, never do I reach higher productivity than after being idle for a while.
在 HN 中查看
Chrisszz 2026/04/11 21:12
是的，我同意，但是如果你有工作要做并且有最后期限，你就不能休息直到交付正确的结果吗？
Yes, I agree, but if you have work to do and a deadline you can't afford resting until you deliver right?
在 HN 中查看
embedding-shape 2026/04/11 21:44
好吧，我再次想说，如果您确实需要在截止日期前完成任务，那么在这种情况下您就不能不休息。

大概每个程序员都经历过“整天被问题撞得头破血流，睡了一觉醒来问题就简单了”的情况。有时，小睡、散步或做其他事情可能会引发同样的事情，但如果你立即这样做，你就可以跳过整个“一整天都在头疼问题”:)

Well, I'd again say you can't afford to not rest in those cases, if you actually need to meet that deadline.

Probably every programmer have experienced the case of "Banged head against issue entire day, after sleeping and waking up the problem was simple". Sometimes, a nap or a walk or doing something else can trigger that same thing, but you skip the whole "banged head against issue entire day" if you do it immediately :)

在 HN 中查看
seb1204 2026/04/11 21:17
嗯，有时 15 分钟的步行会产生奇迹，你会感到精神焕发，并且可以在接下来的 15 分钟内取得更多成果。
Well, sometimes a 15minute walk will do wonders and you are fresh and can achieve more in the following 15min.
在 HN 中查看
d--b 2026/04/11 15:05
请参阅结构化拖延以获取略有不同的方法。

https://structuredprocrastination.com/

See structured procrastination for a slightly different approach.

https://structuredprocrastination.com/

在 HN 中查看
politelemon 2026/04/11 17:47
参考第一行，怎么可能用海藻跳绳呢？真的有那么强吗？
In reference to the first line, how can it be possible to skip rope with seaweed? Is it really that strong?
在 HN 中查看
layer8 2026/04/11 19:09
海藻有许多不同类型，其中一些（例如海带）的功效非常强大： https://www.instagram.com/p/DQZjvH1iYnB/
There are many different types of seaweed, and some, like kelp, can be very strong: https://www.instagram.com/p/DQZjvH1iYnB/
在 HN 中查看
fluxusars 2026/04/11 20:02
停止最小化最大化。并非一切都必须完美。
Stop minmaxing. Not everything has to be perfect.
在 HN 中查看
DeGoldman 2026/04/11 14:39
如何禁止在屏幕上看到其他查看器光标？使其不可读
How to disable seeing other viewer cursors on screen? Makes it unreadable
在 HN 中查看
maxvij 2026/04/11 15:10
是的，对此感到抱歉。并不是真正为同时许多游客而设计的。我会添加一个限制。 （更新：从博客文章中删除了光标交互）
Yeah, sorry about that. Not really made for many visitors at the same time. I’ll add a limit. (update: removed the cursor interaction from blog posts)
在 HN 中查看
jgdxno 2026/04/11 15:15
这样做的目的是什么？
What's the purpose of having this at all?
在 HN 中查看
maxvij 2026/04/11 15:26
它是对 Figma 的有趣参考，因为它是我的设计作品集。我猜你不是粉丝。著名的！
It’s meant to be a fun reference to Figma, since it’s my design portfolio. I gather you’re not a fan. Noted!
在 HN 中查看
jgdxno 2026/04/11 20:49
我们主要使用 Figma 进行头脑风暴和 RICE 评分，这可能是一项艰苦的工作。因此我的反应不冷不热。我的另一个反应是“酷，他是怎么做到的”。
We mostly use figma for brainstorming and RICE-scoring, which can be hard work. Hence my lukewarm reaction. My other reaction was "cool, how’d he do that".
在 HN 中查看
maxvij 2026/04/12 06:23
我想 Figma 也是一个很好的工具！很高兴听到对互动的积极回应！
I suppose Figma is a good tool for that as well! Glad to hear a positive response to the interaction!
在 HN 中查看

在 HN 中查看
bhotka 2026/04/11 14:55
向下滚动并单击关闭花式光标
scroll down and click turn turn off fancy cursors
在 HN 中查看
throwpoaster 2026/04/11 19:32
如果您患有拖延症，请接受多动症评估。

积极的诊断会改变生活。

If you struggle with procrastination please get assessed for ADHD.

A positive diagnosis is life changing.

在 HN 中查看
vovavili 2026/04/11 19:34
仅当您同意服用兴奋剂药物时才可以。
Only if you are okay with the idea of taking stimulant medication.
在 HN 中查看
margor 2026/04/11 19:52
或者，你必须接受终生的奋斗，并且总是对周围的每个人感到自卑，基本上在你受苦时毫不费力地做事。
Alternatively, you must be okay to have lifelong struggle and always feel inferior to everyone around you basically doing stuff effortlessly while you suffer.
在 HN 中查看
kranner 2026/04/11 20:51
也有治疗多动症的非兴奋剂药物。如果你真的很挣扎，那么暂停判断并实际尝试一段时间可能是值得的。在最坏的情况下，你会回到没有药物治疗的状态。对于许多人来说，潜在的好处值得尝试。
There is non-stimulant medication as well for ADHD. If you're really struggling, it might be worthwhile to suspend judgement and actually try these out for a while. In the worst case you go back to how you were without medication. For many people the potential upside is worth the experiment.
在 HN 中查看
laserlight 2026/04/11 21:28
请注意，不接受这个想法是多动症的症状。与您的精神科医生讨论一下。
Note that not being okay with that idea is a symptom of ADHD. Talk about it with your psychiatrist.
在 HN 中查看
guilhas 2026/04/12 00:18
我部门的几个人最近得到了这个。显然是时尚。但这似乎只是懒惰的许可

如果你知道自己有困难，你需要努力改善自己，小步前进，而不是寻找理由或用药物掩盖它

随着社交媒体、YouTube 的广泛沉迷……频繁出现的世界崩溃新闻、无聊的工作……大多数人都有资格接受某种诊断，但这是误导性的

Several people on my department got that recently. Clearly in fashon. But it seems just a license to be lazy

If you know you have dificulties you need to work on bettering yourself, small steps, not find a justification or mask it with drugs

And with the widespread addiction of social media, youtube... frequent world crumbling news, boring jobs... most people would qualify for some sort of diagnosis, which is misleading
